subject: pathophysiology
title: Pathophysiology — High-Yield Notes
description: Condensed revision notes across 10 systems — renal, electrolyte, acid–base, respiratory, cardiovascular, GI/liver, endocrine, haematology, immunity, and thermoregulation.

sections.title: Renal & Acute Kidney Injury
sections.html:
AKI · CKD · GFR · glomerular syndromes

Acute Kidney Injury (AKI) — 3 buckets

  • Prerenal (hypoperfusion): hypovolaemia, heart failure, sepsis, and severe nephrotic syndrome with marked hypoalbuminaemia (low oncotic pressure → low effective circulating volume). Prolonged prerenal AKI progresses to ATN (acute tubular necrosis).
  • Intrinsic/renal: ATN (ischaemia, cisplatin, contrast), acute interstitial nephritis (omeprazole, NSAIDs), glomerulonephritis.
  • Postrenal (obstruction): relief of obstruction → post-obstructive polyuria driven by osmotic diuresis + resistance to ADH (antidiuretic hormone). Watch for hypovolaemia, not hyperkalaemia.
  • Serum creatinine is still the clinical standard (cystatin C is adjunct, not a replacement). Oliguria is not universal — non-oliguric AKI is common.

NSAID nephrotoxicity

  • Main mechanism = inhibition of intrarenal vasodilatory prostaglandins → unopposed afferent arteriolar vasoconstriction → ↓GFR. Most dangerous when renal perfusion already prostaglandin-dependent (volume depletion, HF, cirrhosis).

Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD)

  • Stage 3a (GFR 45–59) is usually asymptomatic — pruritus, nausea, bleeding tendency (uraemic thrombocytopathy) belong to late stages (4–5), not 3a.
  • Endocrine roles of the kidney: erythropoietin secretion, 1α-hydroxylation of vitamin D (NOT 25-hydroxylation — that's the liver), and renin. PTH is parathyroid, not renal.

GFR & albuminuria

  • GFR is inversely proportional to serum creatinine. Best estimating equations are creatinine-based (CKD-EPI/MDRD); urea clearance is unreliable.
  • Normally a small amount of albumin is excreted. Nephrotic-range = >3.5 g/24 h. Multiple myeloma is the classic overflow proteinuria (Bence-Jones light chains, missed by dipstick).

Nephrotic vs Nephritic

  • Nephrotic: proteinuria >3.5 g, hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, hyperlipidaemia (↑hepatic lipoprotein synthesis), hypercoagulability. Causes: minimal change, membranous, FSGS, diabetic nephropathy.
  • Nephritic: haematuria with dysmorphic RBCs / red-cell casts, hypertension, mild proteinuria, ↓GFR. (Dysmorphic RBCs ≠ nephrotic.)
  • RPGN (rapidly progressive GN) hallmark = crescent formation on biopsy.
  • Diabetic nephropathy: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules; mechanisms = hyperglycaemia, RAAS, hypertension, microvascular haemodynamic change, genetics (and VEGF).

sections.title: Electrolytes & Body Water
sections.html:
Na⁺ · K⁺ · Ca²⁺ · ADH · compartments

Body water compartments

  • Total body water ≈ 60% body weight; greater in men than women. ICF = 2/3 (~40% BW), ECF = 1/3 (~20% BW). ECF splits into interstitial (¾) + intravascular plasma (¼).
  • ADH: short half-life (~15–20 min, not 4–5 h); non-osmotic stimuli (volume, baroreceptors) can override osmotic; vasoconstrictor; ↑water reabsorption in the collecting duct.

Potassium

  • Main intracellular ion. Hyperkalaemia: renal failure, K-sparing diuretics, tumour-lysis, hypoaldosteronism, acidosis (β-agonists/aldosterone lower it).
  • Hypokalaemia: hyperaldosteronism, loop/thiazide diuretics, vomiting, β-agonists. (Aldosteronism — primary or secondary — never causes hyperkalaemia.)

Sodium

  • Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia: urine Na may be > or < 20 mmol/L depending on the cause — renal loss (diuretics, salt-losing nephropathy, adrenal insufficiency) gives high UNa; extrarenal loss gives low UNa. ADH is stimulated, not suppressed.
  • Hypervolaemic hyponatraemia: oedematous states — cirrhosis, heart failure, nephrotic syndrome. (CKD is not a cause of hypovolaemic hyponatraemia.)
  • Hyperglycaemia (glucose >200) → translocational/pseudo-hyponatraemia.

Calcium — hypercalcaemia

  • Polyuria + nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, constipation, shortened QT (not prolonged), hypertension, stones/bones/groans. PTH is not always high (low in malignancy-driven hypercalcaemia).

sections.title: Acid–Base Disorders
sections.html:
AG · compensation · Kussmaul · mixed

Core framework

  • Buffers act in minutes (lungs) → hours (kidney). Bicarbonate is the dominant ECF buffer (Henderson-Hasselbalch). Volatile acid (CO₂) cleared by lungs; fixed acids by kidney.
  • Cannot coexist: respiratory acidosis + respiratory alkalosis (the lungs can't simultaneously over- and under-ventilate). Metabolic + respiratory disorders of the same or opposite direction can coexist (mixed).

Metabolic acidosis

  • High anion gap (HAGMA): lactic acidosis, ketoacidosis (DKA), uraemia, toxins (methanol, ethylene glycol, salicylate).
  • Normal anion gap (NAGMA): diarrhoea (GI HCO₃⁻ loss) and renal tubular acidosis (RTA).
  • Kussmaul respiration = deep, rapid breathing — respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis.

Metabolic alkalosis

  • Classic cause: loss of gastric fluid (vomiting → loss of HCl). Also diuretics and hyperaldosteronism (H⁺ + K⁺ wasting → alkalosis + hypokalaemia).

sections.title: Respiratory System
sections.html:
Failure types · V/Q · COPD · PE · effusion

Respiratory failure

  • Type 1 (hypoxaemic): failure of oxygenation — pneumonia, ARDS, pulmonary oedema, PE, fibrosis (parenchymal/V-Q problems).
  • Type 2 (hypercapnic): failure of ventilation — depressed respiratory drive, chest-wall/neuromuscular disease (kyphosis), airway obstruction (bronchitis/COPD). Stimulation of the respiratory centre causes hyper-, not hypo-ventilation.
  • Hypercapnia source = central hypoventilation (shunt, low V/Q, low FiO₂ → hypoxaemia primarily).
  • Hyperventilation → respiratory alkalosis → paraesthesias, confusion, cerebral vasoconstriction (not dilation).

V/Q distribution (upright lung)

  • Apex: high V/Q — over-ventilated relative to perfusion (wasted ventilation). Base: low V/Q — perfusion exceeds ventilation. Both V and Q rise toward the base, but perfusion rises more steeply.

Obstructive vs restrictive

  • COPD: >40–50 yo, heavy smoking (>20–30 pack-years), ↓FEV1/FVC, poor bronchodilator reversibility. Smoking is the main risk factor.
  • Pulmonary fibrosis (restrictive): ↓ volumes, ↓TLC, ↓FRC, normal or increased FEV1/FVC ratio.
  • Asthma: bronchoconstriction + airway inflammation, bronchial hyper-responsiveness, mast cells, mediated by nitric oxide; linked to the ADAM33 gene. Chronic bronchitis = mucus hypersecretion.

PE & pleural effusion

  • Acute PE initial gases: respiratory alkalosis, hypocapnia, hypoxaemia (respiratory acidosis is NOT an early finding).
  • Heart failure effusion = bilateral transudate. Effusions form from ↑hydrostatic pressure, ↓oncotic pressure, ↑permeability, lymphatic block, or iatrogenic causes.
  • Obesity hypoventilation: ↓lung volumes, ↓compliance, ↑work of breathing, and impaired ventilatory response to hypercapnia/hypoxia.

sections.title: Cardiovascular System
sections.html:
HF · endothelium · atherosclerosis · arrhythmia

Heart failure

  • Main symptoms: exertional dyspnoea + easy fatigability. Dyspnoea ← ↑pulmonary capillary (LV end-diastolic) pressure; fatigue ← reduced cardiac output.
  • Mechanisms: ↓contractility & LV dilatation, ↓compliance, ↑LVEDP, ↑ (not ↓) peripheral resistance via SNS/RAAS, and remodelling (ellipsoid → spherical).
  • Signs: JVD, peripheral oedema, basal crackles, S3, displaced apex. Low systolic BP / high BP are not themselves signs. Intense athletic activity is NOT a cause of HF.

Endothelial dysfunction & atherosclerosis

  • Defined as reduced bioavailability of nitric oxide (NO). Healthy endothelium vasodilates to acetylcholine; dysfunctional endothelium vasoconstricts.
  • Unstable plaque: thin fibrous cap, large lipid core, macrophage/lymphocyte infiltration.
  • MI pathophysiology: plaque rupture → thrombus formation. Coronary flow reserve falls significantly once luminal stenosis exceeds ~70%.

Hypertension & arterial stiffness

  • Increased Na intake raises BP in salt-sensitive individuals. Angiotensin II → vasoconstriction, thirst, Na retention, sympathetic activation (NOT natriuresis/vasodilation).
  • Arterial stiffness → ↑systolic BP, ↓diastolic BP, widened pulse pressure, ↑LV afterload.

Arrhythmias

  • SA node initiates the impulse. Atrial fibrillation = irregularly irregular, absent P waves. Most common PSVT = AVNRT (re-entrant looping circuit reactivating tissue).

Lipoproteins

  • Built from apoproteins + lipid. Pathways: exogenous, endogenous, reverse cholesterol transport. Chylomicronaemia = complete LPL deficiency → pancreatitis risk. Secondary dyslipidaemia: hypothyroidism, T2DM.

sections.title: GI, Liver & Pancreas
sections.html:
Malabsorption · ulcers · diarrhoea · cirrhosis

Malabsorption — by phase

  • Intraluminal (digestive): pancreatic enzyme deficiency, bile-salt deficiency, bacterial overgrowth, short bowel, Zollinger-Ellison (acid inactivates enzymes).
  • Mucosal: coeliac disease, Crohn's, parasites, drugs, disaccharidase deficiency. (Intestinal ischaemia is a transport/vascular issue, not mucosal-phase.)

Ulcers & oesophagus

  • Gastric ulcer: acid usually normal or low, defective mucosal defence; can be malignant; H. pylori contributes.
  • Duodenal ulcer: increased acid, relieved by food, essentially never malignant, strongly H. pylori-related.
  • Achalasia: loss of myenteric inhibitory neurons → failure of LES relaxation + aperistalsis; manometry shows simultaneous (spontaneous) contractions; 'bird-beak' on barium. Diffuse oesophageal spasm: normal ganglia, thickened muscle wall, chest pain, dysphagia.

Diarrhoea & ileus

  • Secretory: large volume, persists with fasting, no osmotic gap (e.g. cholera). Osmotic: stops with fasting, osmotic gap, e.g. disaccharidase deficiency.
  • Obstructive ileus: may cause hypovolaemia/shock; proximal obstruction → metabolic alkalosis (vomiting), distal → acidosis.

Liver & biliary

  • Cirrhosis/ascites: requires portal hypertension; splanchnic vasodilation driven by nitric oxide → activation of Na-retaining neurohormonal systems. Portal HTN = ↑hepatic resistance + ↑splanchnic inflow.
  • Bile acids: undergo enterohepatic circulation, needed for fat & fat-soluble vitamin absorption, promote bile flow. Choledocholithiasis: stones can form in ducts too, not only the gallbladder.
  • Neonatal jaundice: physiologic = unconjugated (↑production, ↓uptake, ↓conjugation/immature UGT). Impaired excretion → conjugated (pathologic cholestasis).

sections.title: Endocrine & Diabetes
sections.html:
T1/T2DM · Cushing · GH · prolactin · DI

Diabetes mellitus

  • Type 1: autoimmune β-cell destruction → insulin deficiency; autoantibodies = anti-islet, anti-insulin, anti-GAD (also anti-IA2/tyrosine phosphatase, anti-ZnT8). Associated with other autoimmunity (Hashimoto). Environmental triggers: early cow's-milk exposure, short breastfeeding, nitrosamines.
  • Type 2: diabetogenic genes cause both insulin resistance AND impaired secretion; strong family-history influence (genetics matter more than in T1DM). NAFLD/MASLD reflects insulin resistance.
  • Insulin actions: ↑glucose uptake (GLUT4), ↑glycogenesis, ↑lipogenesis, ↓lipolysis. Fasting glucose ≥126 mg/dL diagnoses diabetes.
  • Complication drivers: duration, glycaemic control & 'metabolic memory', heredity (sex is not a major factor).

Hormone receptors

  • Intracellular receptors: steroids (cortisol, aldosterone, sex steroids), thyroid hormone, vitamin D. Membrane receptors: insulin, glucagon, PTH, catecholamines (peptides/amines).

Pituitary / adrenal

  • GH excess: gigantism (children) / acromegaly (adults) — joint & cartilage change, diabetogenic (hyperglycaemia), and acromegalic cardiomyopathy.
  • Cushing disease = pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma. Cushing syndrome (any cause): moon face, central obesity, striae, buffalo hump, hyperglycaemia, hypertension.
  • Hyperprolactinaemia: not always an adenoma — stress, hypothyroidism, dopamine antagonists; causes galactorrhoea + suppresses gonadotropins. First-line treatment of a prolactinoma = dopamine agonist (cabergoline/bromocriptine).
  • Diabetes insipidus: polyuria of dilute urine + polydipsia; central = low ADH, nephrogenic = ADH resistance. SIADH: inappropriate ADH → euvolaemic hyponatraemia, concentrated urine.

sections.title: Haematology
sections.html:
Anaemias · haemostasis · leukaemias

Anaemia classification

  • Microcytic: iron deficiency, thalassaemia, anaemia of chronic disease. Macrocytic: B12 / folate (megaloblastic). Normocytic: aplastic, acute blood loss.
  • Iron studies: IDA → low ferritin; thalassaemia → low MCV/MCH with normal/high ferritin; anaemia of chronic disease → hepcidin-driven iron sequestration.
  • B12 vs folate: both megaloblastic; B12 deficiency adds neurological signs (subacute combined degeneration) and ↑methylmalonic acid.
  • Acute blood loss: initially Hb/MCV/MCH appear normal (haemoconcentration) before dilution drops Hb.

Haemolysis

  • Direct Coombs positive in autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (a Type II hypersensitivity). Sickle cell = HbS (β-chain point mutation). Findings: ↑LDH, ↑indirect bilirubin, ↓haptoglobin, reticulocytosis.

Haemostasis

  • Platelet lifespan ≈ 7–10 days; RBC = 120 days. Hereditary bleeding: haemophilia A/B, von Willebrand. Acquired: DIC.
  • Haemophilia A — most common bleed = haemarthrosis. Acquired thrombophilia: nephrotic syndrome, antiphospholipid syndrome, myeloproliferative disease (Factor V Leiden is hereditary).

Marrow & malignancy

  • Haematopoiesis stimulators = EPO, TPO, G-CSF; inhibitors = TNF, interferons. Myelocytes are immature (not mature blood cells).
  • AML → Auer rods. Most common adult leukaemia = CLL. Hodgkin lymphoma → Reed-Sternberg cells. Polycythaemia vera = myeloproliferative. Ineffective thrombopoiesis (B12 deficiency, marrow infiltration, aplasia) ≠ hypersplenism (sequestration).

sections.title: Immunity, Inflammation & CTD
sections.html:
Innate/adaptive · hypersensitivity · cartilage

Innate vs adaptive

  • Innate: fast, non-specific, no memory — physical barriers (skin, mucus, cilia), neutrophils, monocytes/macrophages, NK cells, complement. Adaptive: slow, specific, has memory — B cells (antibodies), T cells. Antibodies are NOT innate.

Hypersensitivity

  • I — immediate/IgE (atopy, anaphylaxis). II — cytotoxic/antibody (autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, anti-GBM). III — immune complex (SLE, post-strep GN). IV — delayed/T-cell (Mantoux/PPD test, contact dermatitis).

Inflammation & cytokines

  • Acute inflammation: vasodilation (not vasoconstriction), ↑permeability/oedema, heat, redness, pain. Pro-inflammatory cytokines: TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6 (fever, anorexia, catabolism, tachycardia).
  • Vasoactive mediators: histamine, serotonin, adenosine (ferritin is NOT). Exogenous pyrogen = Gram-negative endotoxin (LPS); endogenous = IL-1, TNF, interferons.
  • SLE: autoimmune, multisystem, immune-complex (Type III) → typically thrombocytopenia (not thrombocytosis).

Connective tissue / cartilage

  • Articular cartilage: Type II collagen, avascular/aneural, basic unit = chondron; compressive resistance from hydrophilic proteoglycan GAG chains.
  • Seronegative spondyloarthropathies = axial skeleton, RF-negative. Rheumatoid arthritis spares the DIP joints. Marfan affects eye, skeleton, skin & CV — leading cause of death = aortic dissection/rupture.

sections.title: Thermoregulation, Sleep & Skin
sections.html:
Temperature · sleep stages · skin

Thermoregulation

  • Heat loss: vasodilation, sweating, insensible respiration (vasoconstriction conserves heat). Hypothermia in hypothyroidism, malnutrition, alcohol — not hyperthyroidism (heat intolerance).

Sleep

  • Normal sleep begins with NREM (3 stages, ~90–120 min cycles), not REM. REM = dreaming, wake-like EEG, muscle atonia.

Skin (high-yield)

  • Atopic dermatitis: Type I-associated, impaired barrier, the 'itch that rashes'. Melanoma ABCDE; Type 1 diabetes beta-cell destruction shown by skin/systemic autoimmune associations.
